original_abstract:
We report the development of a highly enantioselective, radical-based hydroamination of enol esters with sulfonamides, facilitated by a synergistic catalytic system comprising an iridium photocatalyst, a Brønsted base, and a chiral tetrapeptide thiol. This method provides efficient access to a broad range of protected $\beta$-amino alcohol derivatives—23 examples in total—with enantiomeric ratios reaching up to 97:3. The critical stereodefining step involves an asymmetric hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) from the cysteine-bearing peptide catalyst to a prochiral, carbon-centered radical intermediate.

Comprehensive structure-selectivity investigations into both the peptide framework and the alkene substrate provided the foundation for optimizing the catalyst architecture, particularly highlighting the role of the $i+2$ and $i+3$ residues. Experimental kinetic studies and Hammett analysis, supported by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, reveal that stereocontrol is governed by a sophisticated network of noncovalent interactions. Specifically, inter- and intramolecular hydrogen bonding, $\pi$-$\pi$ stacking, and London dispersion interactions work in concert to orient the radical species within the peptide's binding pocket. These results advance the scope of catalytic asymmetric HAT and offer deeper insights into the use of modular peptides to control the reactivity of open-shell intermediates via noncovalent stabilization.
humanized_abstract:
Herein, we disclose a highly enantioselective radical hydroamination of enol esters with sulfonamides by means of a complementary iridium photocatalyst/Bronsted acid/tetrapeptide thiol catalyst. By using this combination of catalysts, the targeted $\beta$-amino ester products—obtained in good to excellent yields for 23 examples—can be obtained with enhanced enantiocontrol up to 97:3. Critically, this catalytic system achieves total control of the crucial hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) stereochemistry, involving a chiral cysteine containing peptide to hybridize and subsequently reduce the prochiral carbon-centred radical species involved in the key hydroamination event.

A detailed structural optimization of the peptide substrate and alkene reactivity was carried out to establish a basis for design of catalysts with optimal performance, based on the roles of the $i+2$ and $i+3$ amino acids. Kinetic studies and Hammett analysis, supported by density functional theory calculations, showed that the stereochemistry of the reaction is controlled by a complex interplay of non-covalent interactions including inter- and intramolecular hydrogen bonds, $\pi$-$\pi$ stacking and London dispersion forces that direct the radical species in the binding site of the peptide catalyst. In all, this work significantly advances the field of catalytic asymmetric hydroaromatic transfer (HAT) and provides detailed insight into the modular peptide approach to controlling the reactivity of open-shell intermediates via non-covalent binding.

document.input:
We report the development of a highly enantioselective, radical-based hydroamination of enol esters with sulfonamides, facilitated by a synergistic catalytic system comprising an iridium photocatalyst, a Brønsted base, and a chiral tetrapeptide thiol.
